doc.title: Accruals — earned or incurred first, cash later
doc.event: Two more December 31 adjustments: employees earned $500 of wages in late December (payday is Jan 3), and the studio finished $800 of work in December that won't be billed until January. Prepare both accruals.

entries.caption: Dec 31: $500 of wages earned by employees, payday Jan 3

lines.label: December's cost in December
lines.why: The work happened this year — matching puts the cost in this year, payday or not.
lines.trap: Waiting for payday shoves December's cost into January — the exact error adjustments exist to prevent.

lines.label: owed, not paid
lines.trap: Jan 3's entry is then Dr Wages Payable / Cr Cash — NOT expense again. The follow-through is where the second point is won or lost.

entries.caption: Dec 31: $800 of December work, billing in January

lines.label: earned = owed to you
lines.why: The claim exists because the work is done — the invoice is paperwork, not the earning event.
lines.trap: 'We haven't billed yet' doesn't delay revenue. Earned in December = December's revenue.

lines.label: December's earnings in December
lines.why: The mirror of the wage accrual — same logic, revenue side.
lines.trap: All four adjustment types are the same idea: put the income effect in the period it BELONGS, wherever the cash is.

memorize.body: Accrual = income effect FIRST, cash LATER. Accrued expense: Dr Expense / Cr Payable. Accrued revenue: Dr Receivable / Cr Revenue. Later cash settles the payable/receivable — no second income hit.
The four-type map: cash-first = deferrals (prepaid, unearned) · cash-later = accruals (accrued expense, accrued revenue). Every adjusting entry you'll ever see is one of the four.

questions.prompt: Wages of $500 were accrued Dec 31; payday is Jan 3. The Jan 3 entry debits…

distractors.feedback: Debiting Wages Expense on payday double-counts December's cost. The accrual already expensed it; Jan 3 is Dr Wages Payable 500 / Cr Cash 500.

build.scaffold: Ask: has the economic event happened (work done, cost incurred)? Then it goes in THIS period — pair it with a payable or receivable and leave Cash alone.
